name: TypeScript Interview Foundations — additive questions
description: Question expansion derived from the attached source files; reuses existing repository topic IDs and defines only missing concepts.

questions.title: Applying Type Composition in TypeScript in practice
questions.prompt: A discriminated union models loading, success, and error states; a tuple models a fixed-position pair. What conclusion would you draw, which assumptions matter, and how would you validate it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Distinguishes union and intersection
expectedPoints.description: Explains alternative versus combined requirements.

expectedPoints.label: Explains tuple semantics
expectedPoints.description: Mentions typed positions and fixed structure.

expectedPoints.label: Uses domain-driven examples
expectedPoints.description: Shows how the constructs prevent invalid states.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Type Composition in TypeScript
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Using an intersection when the domain means either-or." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Distinguishes union and intersection
expectedPoints.description: Explains alternative versus combined requirements.

expectedPoints.label: Explains tuple semantics
expectedPoints.description: Mentions typed positions and fixed structure.

expectedPoints.label: Uses domain-driven examples
expectedPoints.description: Shows how the constructs prevent invalid states.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Using a broad array where position and length are part of the contract?

questions.title: Applying Structural typing and assignability in practice
questions.prompt: A function expecting an object with id and name can receive a richer object that contains at least those members. What conclusion would you draw, which assumptions matter, and how would you validate it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Defines structural compatibility
expectedPoints.description: States that required members, not names, drive compatibility.

expectedPoints.label: Explains practical flexibility
expectedPoints.description: Mentions composition, substitution, or richer objects.

expectedPoints.label: Recognizes semantic risk
expectedPoints.description: Notes same-shape domain values or suggests branding.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Structural typing and assignability
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Saying two interfaces must explicitly extend each other to be compatible." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Defines structural compatibility
expectedPoints.description: States that required members, not names, drive compatibility.

expectedPoints.label: Explains practical flexibility
expectedPoints.description: Mentions composition, substitution, or richer objects.

expectedPoints.label: Recognizes semantic risk
expectedPoints.description: Notes same-shape domain values or suggests branding.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Ignoring semantic differences between values that share a shape?

questions.title: Applying TypeScript generics and constraints in practice
questions.prompt: A first<T>(items: T[]) function returns T, preserving whether the caller supplied users, strings, or numbers. What conclusion would you draw, which assumptions matter, and how would you validate it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Preserves type relationships
expectedPoints.description: Explains that generics retain caller-specific information.

expectedPoints.label: Contrasts with any
expectedPoints.description: States that any discards checking and useful inference.

expectedPoints.label: Explains constraints
expectedPoints.description: Uses extends to require a capability without fixing one concrete type.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about TypeScript generics and constraints
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Using any where a generic relationship should be preserved." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Preserves type relationships
expectedPoints.description: Explains that generics retain caller-specific information.

expectedPoints.label: Contrasts with any
expectedPoints.description: States that any discards checking and useful inference.

expectedPoints.label: Explains constraints
expectedPoints.description: Uses extends to require a capability without fixing one concrete type.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Adding generic parameters that appear only once and express no relationship?

questions.title: Applying Type Declaration and Inheritance Choices in practice
questions.prompt: A component prop shape can be a type alias; a public service capability can be an interface implemented by different classes. What conclusion would you draw, which assumptions matter, and how would you validate it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Compares interface and alias
expectedPoints.description: Mentions shared object modeling and relevant differences.

expectedPoints.label: Separates implements and extends
expectedPoints.description: Distinguishes contract checking from inheriting implementation.

expectedPoints.label: Prefers composition appropriately
expectedPoints.description: Warns against deep hierarchies and models small capabilities.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Type Declaration and Inheritance Choices
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Claiming interface and type are identical in every language feature." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Compares interface and alias
expectedPoints.description: Mentions shared object modeling and relevant differences.

expectedPoints.label: Separates implements and extends
expectedPoints.description: Distinguishes contract checking from inheriting implementation.

expectedPoints.label: Prefers composition appropriately
expectedPoints.description: Warns against deep hierarchies and models small capabilities.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Using inheritance only to reuse code without a genuine is-a relationship?

questions.title: Applying Declaration files and library typing in practice
questions.prompt: A JavaScript package can ship index.d.ts so TypeScript users get autocomplete and checking without rewriting the package. What conclusion would you draw, which assumptions matter, and how would you validate it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Defines declaration files
expectedPoints.description: States that they describe existing runtime APIs for TypeScript.

expectedPoints.label: Separates types from runtime
expectedPoints.description: Explains that declarations emit no implementation.

expectedPoints.label: Recognizes accuracy risk
expectedPoints.description: Notes false safety or mismatch with the JavaScript library.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Declaration files and library typing
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Expecting a declaration file to polyfill or implement missing runtime behavior." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Defines declaration files
expectedPoints.description: States that they describe existing runtime APIs for TypeScript.

expectedPoints.label: Separates types from runtime
expectedPoints.description: Explains that declarations emit no implementation.

expectedPoints.label: Recognizes accuracy risk
expectedPoints.description: Notes false safety or mismatch with the JavaScript library.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Using broad any declarations that silence useful errors?
